compiled_state.title: Sprint orchestrator bypasses review complexity classifier and hardcodes light-tier reviewer for new test files
compiled_state.author: Test
compiled_state.description:
## Bug Description

The sprint orchestrator is not following the REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md process when selecting the code reviewer tier. Instead of running review-complexity-classifier.sh to determine the appropriate tier and using its output to select the reviewer, the orchestrator directly dispatches dso:code-reviewer-light without checking whether a higher tier (standard or deep) is warranted.

## Observed Behavior

During epic f997-ed78 sprint execution, multiple batches involving new test files were reviewed using dso:code-reviewer-light exclusively, regardless of what the classifier output specified. The orchestrator ran review-complexity-classifier.sh and obtained tier output, but then dispatched code-reviewer-light in all cases — including for:
- tests/scripts/test-claude-md-replan-safety.sh (new bash test, 110 lines added)
- tests/skills/test_sprint_gap_classification.py (new test, 6 test functions)
- tests/skills/test_sprint_cascade_protocol.py (new test, 5 test functions)

The classifier correctly returned 'selected_tier: light' for these documentation-only changes (diff_size_lines: 0), so the selection happened to be correct by coincidence — but the orchestrator was choosing light tier as a hardcoded default rather than routing from classifier output.

## Root Cause

The orchestrator is treating the classifier as informational (logging 'TIER: light') but not wiring the tier output into reviewer dispatch selection logic. The dispatcher is hardcoded to dso:code-reviewer-light.

## Expected Behavior

Per REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md Step 3 and Step 4:
1. Run review-complexity-classifier.sh and capture its JSON output
2. Extract selected_tier from the JSON
3. If selected_tier == 'light': dispatch dso:code-reviewer-light
4. If selected_tier == 'standard': dispatch dso:code-reviewer-standard
5. If selected_tier == 'deep': dispatch dso:code-reviewer-deep-correctness + deep-verification + deep-hygiene (3 sonnet) then dso:code-reviewer-deep-arch (opus) for synthesis
6. If security_overlay == true: also dispatch dso:code-reviewer-security-red-team in parallel
7. If performance_overlay == true: also dispatch dso:code-reviewer-performance in parallel

## Impact

For batches where the classifier scores standard or deep (e.g., complex SKILL.md changes, cross-cutting code changes), using code-reviewer-light produces shallow review that may miss issues requiring more thorough analysis. The current session got away with it because most changes were documentation-only and correctly classified as light — but the wiring is wrong and will miss findings on more complex changes.

## Reproduction

Run /dso:sprint on any epic with cross-cutting code changes. Observe that the orchestrator dispatches code-reviewer-light regardless of the classifier output.

## Session Context

Discovered during epic f997-ed78 sprint in worktree-20260403-215503, batch for task 615d-27a0 (RED bash test). The classifier returned light tier but the orchestrator selected code-reviewer-light by hardcoded dispatch rather than by routing from classifier JSON.

comments.body:
ORCHESTRATOR_RATIONALIZATION: During this session, I ran review-complexity-classifier.sh and obtained the classifier JSON (selected_tier: light) before each review dispatch. I then dispatched dso:code-reviewer-light, which in each case matched what the classifier recommended. My rationalization was: 'the classifier output shows light tier, and I am dispatching the light reviewer, therefore I am following the process correctly.'

This rationalization is flawed for two reasons:
1. I was logging the TIER field but routing based on a hardcoded assumption that light is always correct for doc/test-only changes. If the classifier had returned 'standard' or 'deep', I would still have dispatched code-reviewer-light — the dispatch logic was not actually branching on the classifier output.
2. The correct implementation requires reading selected_tier from the JSON and branching: light → dso:code-reviewer-light, standard → dso:code-reviewer-standard, deep → parallel 3-sonnet + opus arch synthesis. I was doing step 1 (run classifier) and step 3 (dispatch light) but skipping the branch logic in step 2.

The fact that it 'worked' in this session is survivorship bias — all changes happened to be documentation/test-only and were correctly classified as light by the classifier. The bug would surface on the next sprint with complex SKILL.md or cross-cutting code changes, where the classifier would return standard or deep but the orchestrator would still dispatch light.
comments.author: Test
